p: How the AWS EKS fleet is built, run, and extended — cluster provisioning, the app onboarding & deploy pipeline, the platform stack, and the services underneath. Region: us-east-2 · Clusters: 3 (dev / qa / prod) · Kubernetes: 1.35 on Bottlerocket · Provisioner: Terragrunt → Octopus · Source: variant-inc/iaac-eks.

ac:rich-text-body.p: Scope: AWS cloud EKS only. On-prem Talos (op-usxpress-dev/qa) is a separate architecture. Baseline confirmed live 2026-07-15; component versions sampled from usxpress-dev. Interactive (styled) version: the Claude artifact — open.

p: Three single-tenant EKS clusters, one per AWS account, all in us-east-2. Everything is provisioned as code and promoted dev → qa → prod.

tr.td: 1.35 on all three envs (platform eks.15–17), one minor behind newest 1.36. Insights PASS except a kube-proxy skew warning.
Bottlerocket OS 1.62.1 (aws-k8s-1.35), kernel 6.12.88, containerd 2.1.8, kubelet v1.35.4 — Graviton m7g/m6g.xlarge, AMI type BOTTLEROCKET_ARM_64.

p: Isolation is by AWS account, not namespace. A shared services account holds the image & chart registry consumed by all three clusters.

tr.td: First landing zone for every change; RisingWave & new addons prove out here.
Pre-prod validation tier in the Octopus lifecycle.
Production workloads; ~150 Octopus projects in the USXpress space.
Image + Helm-chart registry (ECR) pulled by all clusters.

ac:rich-text-body.p: INFRA-1594's fleet-upgrade plan references a dev → QA/staging → prod flow — a distinct staging tier may be introduced for the upgrade rollout. Today the running fleet is the three clusters above.

p: Clusters are provisioned from variant-inc/iaac-eks (default branch master). Nothing is applied from a laptop — a GitHub Action packages the release and Octopus runs it.

Flow: iaac-eks/apps/terragrunt (Terragrunt) + PowerShell → GitHub Actions packages the release → Octopus DevOps space → Terragrunt run per env (EKS control plane + Karpenter node config) → iaac-eks-bootstrap lays in networking, security, DNS, certs.

ac:rich-text-body.p: The KT-era "deploy/terraform + modules/" layout is outdated. In iaac-eks the deploy/ dir holds orchestration only (config.yaml, secrets.yaml, deploy.ps1); the actual IaC lives under apps/ — Terragrunt units in apps/terragrunt (root.hcl, _envcommon, modules/, live/), Helm via Helmfile in apps/charts.

tr.td: Cluster + app infrastructure — Terragrunt units in apps/terragrunt + Helm charts in apps/charts, driven by PowerShell (deploy/deploy.ps1).

Core platform bootstrap — networking, security, DNS, certificate management. A Helmfile with a dir per component: cilium, cert-manager, external-dns, external-secrets, flux, karpenter, aws-load-balancer, reloader, vpa, kube-system, pre_helm, common.
Reusable Terraform module for AWS node groups.
Flux GitOps source for the platform layer.
SRE scripts deployed across all EKS clusters.
OpenTelemetry operator, Honeycomb agent, observability tooling.
Prowler security scans → AWS Security Hub.

h2: Terragrunt layout & where the addons come from

td.p: live/ wires each env to modules/ with _envcommon defaults. Helm is layered via Helmfile in apps/charts (helmfiles.d, values, bases).
A Helmfile (helmfile.yaml) with a directory per component — this is where the §5 stack is actually authored:

p.code: cilium · cert-manager · external-dns · external-secrets · flux · karpenter · aws-load-balancer · reloader · vpa · kube-system · pre_helm · common

p: Karpenter provisions the entire fleet just-in-time. All three clusters run K8s 1.35; newest available is 1.36 (one minor behind — healthy).

p.em: * Every UPGRADE_READINESS insight PASSES except kube-proxy version skew (WARNING) on all three. Upgrade policy EXTENDED = clusters may roll into paid extended support rather than auto-upgrade — a cost/governance choice, not a sign they're unsupported.

p: Nodes: Karpenter 1.11.1 owns the whole fleet — every node carries a karpenter.sh/nodepool label; none run on an EKS managed node group. NodePools observed: iaac (platform), apps (workloads), ingressgateway (Istio). Node image Bottlerocket 1.62.1 / Graviton ARM64 (m7g/m6g.xlarge).

ac:rich-text-body.p: cluster-autoscaler is vestigial — still deployed (cluster-autoscaler-aws-cluster-autoscaler, image v1.26.2, 9 minors stale) but every node is Karpenter-owned, so it has nothing to scale. Confirm idle, then remove.

p: Addons ship three ways. Delivery reality (live 2026-07-15): only kube-proxy is an AWS-managed EKS addon; vpc-cni, CoreDNS, EBS/EFS CSI are self-managed Helm releases reconciled by Flux (authored as Helmfile in iaac-eks-bootstrap). Cilium 1.19.2 runs alongside the AWS VPC CNI (network-policy / observability).

ul.li: kube-proxy — v1.34.1, one minor behind cluster (skew warning)

td.p: Everything else — authored as Helmfile in iaac-eks-bootstrap, reconciled by Flux:

ul.li: autoscaling — karpenter (owns fleet), keda, descheduler, goldilocks, VPA 1.5.1 (cluster-autoscaler vestigial)

ac:rich-text-body.p: kube-system also carries: the VPA stack (admission/recommender/updater 1.5.1), the VPC-CNI aws-network-policy-agent (v1.3.1), a headroom DaemonSet (pause pods reserving warm capacity for Karpenter), a Windows EBS-CSI node DaemonSet, and homegrown ops controllers (cleanup-oomkilled/pending/stuck-resize-pods, bump-vpa-on-oom, service-topology) running on an unpinned chainguard/kubectl:latest-dev tag — worth pinning.

p: North-south traffic (external clients ↔ the cluster) enters through the aws-load-balancer-controller (v3.1.0, ALB/NLB) + a pair of Istio ingress gateways (public + internal). East-west traffic (service ↔ service inside the cluster) runs on Istio 1.29.2 with the Istio CNI, namespaces labelled istio.io/dataplane-mode: ambient. Monitoring is the full kube-prometheus-stack. Flux reconciles the platform layer.
None of the datastores are AWS-managed services (no MSK / DocumentDB / ElastiCache). They're a mix of external SaaS and on-cluster operators — which decides who owns version upgrades:

tr.td: RDS PostgreSQL 14.22 — one per env (dev/qa t4g.small, prod r6g.large Multi-AZ). CNPG operator installed but unused.
Operator installed (4.4.34) — 0 clusters in any env

p.em: Confirmed across dev + qa + prod: app Postgres is RDS PostgreSQL 14.22 (Graviton; prod Multi-AZ). The CNPG and RabbitMQ operators run 0 clusters and are effectively unused. Kafka brokers are external (Confluent Cloud); Strimzi on-cluster runs Kafka Connect (the connect-connect-* pods), not brokers. PG 14 nears end-of-life — an RDS major upgrade (14 → 16/17) is a due item alongside the K8s one.

p: Pods get AWS credentials through IAM Roles for Service Accounts. On EKS this is native: the OIDC provider and pod-identity webhook are supplied by the managed control plane (nothing to vendor, unlike on-prem Talos).

Flow: SA annotated eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn → EKS pod-identity webhook injects token + role env → sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity → short-lived creds for AWS API calls. Trust-policy subject must be system:serviceaccount:<ns>:<sa> exactly.

An app is defined by a spec.yaml in its own repo (the deployment contract). mage-runner reads it, validates against JSON schemas, and drives terraform-variant-apps to build the app's infra + deploy its Helm chart.

ul.li: common/ — eks-data, role, replicator, auth, buckets, kafka, postgres, dynamodb, mongodb-cluster, mongodb-user, mongodb-privatelink
apps/ — exactly one of api / cron / handler / ui

p: One chart per app type from ECR: helm-charts/dx-{api,cron,handler,ui} — provisions Deployment, Service, ServiceAccount (IRSA-annotated), and the Istio VirtualService/Gateway. Per-app state: s3://{bucket}/{space}/{app}/{module}/terraform.tfstate.

Pipeline: git push (app repo) → GitHub Actions builds + tests, pushes image to ECR (064859874041) → mage-runner creates an Octopus release with the new tag → Octopus USXpress space — click Deploy (dev → qa → prod) → mage-runner CD drives terraform-variant-apps → infra + Helm chart → pod running.
"DX" is the whole deployment platform, not a single repo. Three layers, easy to conflate:

tr.td: mage-runner + Octopus library variable sets (DX__Common, DX__EKSCluster, DX__Runner, DX__TFState, DX__AWSAccounts) + the DX-Apply step + GitHub Actions workflows.
A specific Octopus deployment-process step (inline PowerShell) that prepares the environment and invokes mage. (On-prem, a patched version reads SSM and strips spec.yaml.)
The actual Go binary that does the work — reads the spec, decides which Terraform modules to run, runs them, composes Helm values, talks to Octopus.

h2: 8b. Two repos, and how they connect

tr.td: The orchestrator — Go, built with the Mage tool. Fork branch feature/onprem-support.
The Terraform modules mage invokes (~40 modules across common/infrastructure/apps).

p: The link: mage-runner builds a path into $TERRAFORM_APPS_PATH/modules/<module> and runs each module through the hashicorp/terraform-exec Go SDK — it never shells out to the terraform CLI.
h2: 8c. Where the Go lives — mage-runner folder structure
p: Go 1.22+, built with Mage (a Go-based Make): go build -o /usr/local/bin/mage ./cmd/mage. Entry point is cmd/mage/magefiles/terraform.go → TfDeploy.Apply().

ac:structured-macro.ac:plain-text-body:
mage-runner/ |- cmd/mage/magefiles/ | |- terraform.go # TfDeploy: Apply / Destroy / Infrastructure / Apps / ScriptRun | |- common.go # Prepare, ParseSpec, ValidateSpec | |- octopus.go # CreateOctopusRelease, project/channel setup | \- github.go # GitHub Actions integration |- internal/ | |- context/ | | |- context.go # DXContext, DXSpec types | | \- env.go # Config struct + env-tag mapping (caarlos0/env) | |- terraform/ # verified live 2026-07-15 | | |- terraform_functions.go # * TfRun() -- the heart of the pipeline | | |- terraform.go terraform_helpers.go | | |- interface.go # TF abstraction constants.go | | |- common.go # shouldRunTerraform(), S3 state management | | |- apps.go apps_functions.go script_run.go | | |- namespace.go replicator.go role.go auth.go | | |- buckets.go kafka.go postgres.go dynamodb.go | | |- mongodb_cluster.go mongodb_user.go | | \- *_test.go test_helpers.go test_mocks.go # full unit-test suite | |- kube/ # namespace.go, apps.go (helm/pod mgmt), common.go (kubeconfig) | |- shared/ # spec.go (YAML parse + env merge), schema_validation.go, functions.go | \- octopus/ # Octopus SDK wrappers |- hack/ # local dev: spec.yaml + <cluster>-terraform.tfvars.json \- go.mod
Mage targets: mage tfDeploy:apply | tfDeploy:destroy | tfDeploy:apps | createOctopusRelease

p: The full pipeline runs in three ordered stages. Each infrastructure module runs only if present in the spec (gated by shouldRunTerraform(), which checks both spec content and existing S3 state).

tr.td: Env + channel guard — a feature channel may only deploy to the dev/qa allow-list.

Each TF module in sequence: replicator → buckets (S3) → auth (Azure AD) → kafka (Confluent) → postgres → dynamodb → mongodb-cluster/user/privatelink → role (IAM/IRSA, always if an app type is defined). The namespace is created by mage via the K8s SDK (internal/kube/namespace.go) — there is no namespace Terraform module.
Exactly ONE of api / cron / handler / ui. Composes Helm values from prior module outputs (tags, labels, role ARN, configmaps, secret refs) and applies the dx-<type> chart.
Runs spec.octopus.postStep, writes artifacts/outputs.yaml, cleans up /tmp.

p: The heart of the engine (internal/terraform/terraform_functions.go). For each module it:

ol.li: Generates *.auto.tfvars.json in the module dir: backend.auto.tfvars.json (S3 backend config), mage.auto.tfvars.json (tags, spec, labels, role_arn, configmaps, aws_secrets), hack.auto.tfvars.json (local overrides).
Stores outputs in GlobalContext.Outputs[module] so later modules consume them.
Persists mage.auto.tfvars.json to S3 (so a later destroy uses the same vars even if the spec changed).

ac:structured-macro.ac:plain-text-body:
terraform-variant-apps/ (invoked per-module by mage, not one big plan) \- modules/ # ONLY two top-level dirs |- common/ | |- eks-data * # cluster endpoint / OIDC / CA -- the hub | |- role # IAM role + IRSA trust | |- replicator # deploy-metadata ConfigMap | |- auth # Azure AD app registration | |- buckets # S3 | |- kafka # Confluent topics + service account | |- postgres dynamodb | \- mongodb-cluster mongodb-user mongodb-privatelink \- apps/ \- api cron handler ui # helm_release of the dx-<type> chart
NOTE: there is NO modules/infrastructure dir, and NO namespace or tags module -- the namespace is created by mage via the K8s SDK, not by Terraform.

tr.td: Reads cluster endpoint / OIDC issuer / CA from the EKS API. The hub every other module consumes — if it can't resolve, nothing deploys.
Per-app IAM role named <cluster>-<app> with an IRSA trust policy binding the SA (system:serviceaccount:<ns>:<sa>) via the OIDC provider. This is the per-app IRSA setup.
helm_release of the dx-{api,cron,handler,ui} chart from ECR — provisions Deployment, Service, IRSA-annotated ServiceAccount, and Istio VirtualService/Gateway.
Writes a <app>-iaac-replicator ConfigMap of deploy metadata; Flux uses it as a drift breadcrumb.

p: Every app repo carries a spec.yaml. mage parses it into the Go DXSpec struct and validates the free-form maps against schemas/.

Env vars (internal/context/env.go, populated by caarlos0/env): S3_BUCKET (from DX__TFState), AWS_REGION, KUBECONFIG (must be absolute), TERRAFORM_APPS_PATH (from DX__Common), OctopusEnvironmentName/ReleaseChannelName/ProjectName, and any TF_VAR_* pass-through.

End-to-end: git push → GitHub Actions build/test → ECR image → mage createOctopusRelease (new release pinned to the image tag) → Octopus USXpress space, click Deploy → DX-Apply step → mage tfDeploy:apply → Infrastructure modules → Apps module → Helm release → pod running.

ul.li: Onboarding = the first release. Author writes spec.yaml; first Apply() creates the namespace, IAM role + IRSA, any S3/Kafka/PG/Dynamo, and the Helm release.
Upgrade = every release after. Same pipeline, new image tag; infra modules are idempotent, the apps module rolls the Deployment. ~200 app releases across the fleet.

p.em: App chart is dx-{api,cron,handler,ui} (2.x); a legacy variant-* chart is mid-migration and accounts for the handful of failed Helm releases. Repo structure verified against the live mage-runner + terraform-variant-apps repos, 2026-07-15 — note this corrects the older KT docs, which described a modules/infrastructure split and namespace/tags modules that do not exist.

tr.td: Cluster role, per-app IRSA roles, OIDC provider, AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity.
Terraform state, app buckets, RisingWave state store.
App + integration secrets; the ESO source of truth.
Primary app Postgres — one PG 14.22 instance per env (prod Multi-AZ); Graviton t4g/r6g.
App table module; historical TF state locking.
Ingress via aws-load-balancer-controller; NLB/PrivateLink for exposed services.
Subnets, security groups, VPC endpoints / PrivateLink.
EKS envelope encryption of etcd secrets (per cluster; INFRA-1507).
Control-plane logs; IRSA / cross-account assume debugging.
DNS automated by external-dns (v0.20.0, active); ACM for certs.
Bump to v1.35.x — clears the only failing insight.
Current for 1.35; re-bakes on Karpenter drift.
Confirm intent — EXTENDED = paid extended support vs auto-upgrade.
Migrate to dx-* charts / clean up.
Confirm idle, then remove — Karpenter owns all nodes.
PG 14 nears EOL — plan RDS major upgrade (→ 16/17).

p.em: Only kube-proxy is an EKS managed addon; vpc-cni / coredns / EBS / EFS are Helm — upgrade = bump the pinned chart in iaac-eks-manifests.

p: Active workstreams: INFRA-1594 (fleet EKS upgrade, owner Mark w/ Rohit + Parul) — now a dual upgrade with RDS PG 14→16/17. INFRA-1507 (EKS etcd envelope-encryption posture, read-only audit).

ol.li: Bump kube-proxy to v1.35.x — clears the one WARNING insight across all three clusters.
Decide support policy — all three are EXTENDED. Keep (buys runway, costs more) or switch to STANDARD before 1.36.
Clean up ~7 failed Helm releases, the vestigial cluster-autoscaler, and pin the latest-dev ops controllers.
Confirm intent on the idle CNPG + RabbitMQ operators (0 clusters in any env).

p.em: Sources: docs/onboarding/idris-kt (sessions 3–7), jira INFRA-1594 / INFRA-1507, and live cluster + repo inspection on 2026-07-15.
